sentences.text: Sorry, OK.
Either one is good.
Anyway, talking by that farmer-- the American officer told this worker at the farm, a Polish-- because, again, they're identified by the P-- take us to the camp.
We didn't know what camp it was.
But anyway, he said, come in a wagon.
He had a pair of horses, on a flat-- flatbed wagon.
We sat down there and he's riding there.
We arrived-- he said, this is the camp.
Its wasn't like any camp what we had before.
It must've been maybe a camp for civilians.
So it was nicer.
It was already houses.
Just one floor, like ranch-- ranch houses.
Not upstairs, just one floor.
And he took us to an office.
And the guy, the Pole, says to the other guy, in Polish, I brought you two people who survived.
You have Polish?
We're home, we can talk.
And my friend Alta goes again with crossing himself.
And the guy recognized the accent that we are Jews, we're not Poles.
So he said, don't worry, nothing is going to happen to you now.
I am the guy who's supplying the food.
I'm running the office and distributing the food for the whole camp here.
We have people from Holland here, we have people from France, we have people from all over from Europe-- Italians here who were prisoners at the-- We assign you.
We got one house which got Jewish people-- we assign you there.
We said, fine.
But in the meantime, he says, take this loaf of bread each.
And he gave us a kielbasa each.
Go-- in the meantime, you'll have something to eat till we all register.
We registered, he took our names, marked us down and he knew already what-- where he assigned us.
So we were there maybe a dozen-- when he came in, we met 10 people like us, Jewish guys.
Now we start talking.
Who are you?
Where were you?
What camps were you?
How did you survive?
And we saw some of these people were from the woods where they shot us up-- in the outer distance, in the outer loop that escaped, that survived.
And one tells me his name is Volvek.
I say, wait a minute, in the woods, a guy with a hat-- I described the hat-- was looking for you, turning the dead people over and hollering Volvek, Volvek.
Was your brother wearing a hat?
I didn't know his name.
Was your brother wearing a hat?
I described.
He said yes, that means he's alive.
I said, he was alive.
He survived the massacre, but where he is now, I don't know.
So he had an idea his brother survived the massacre.
But whether he survived the war, the three, four days after that-- actually, three days, Friday through Monday-- he didn't know.
But eventually, everything fell in line.
He survived somewhere.
One-- one, they bury a Polack.
They helped the Polish people who worked there buried one guy up to the neck and he covered the head-- also in the woods-- from trees.
You know, he covered him, he should be able to breathe.
And he came-- from one day, he brought him food, he fed him.
Because everything else was underground, covered, he came and fed him.
But luckily, it was just for two or three days, you know?
So other people came and we had already a gang of Jewish guys who could talk to each other.
At that time, the war wasn't over.
Because that was April the 15th, a Monday or a Sunday.
I recall her, but that was that time, and war was still on.
So soldiers, American, used to come in.
We had no electricity.
However, we got some candles-- maybe from the office, because when I came, it was already there.
So we got some candles and we lit up candles to see in the evening a little light.
A guy comes in, knock at the door, comes in.
And you couldn't see from the table, short of the candles by the door, who's coming in.
He comes in-- [NON-ENGLISH].
Shut off, because German planes are still in the air.
And we can't have lights on.
And to me, his German sounded like Yiddish.
And I said to that Volvek, what his brother was looking for him, I say, he sounds like Yiddish.
Let's ask him.
This guy, the soldier, heard the word Yiddish.
He came over-- you speak Yiddish?
I said, yes.
Now he spoke in Jewish and said, juden?
I said, yes.
He said, ich bin Yiddish.
Ich bin von der Bronx, New York.
My tateh, my father is a cantor and a mohel.
My namen is Walter Schrader.
So I said, I can  never forget this name-- the first American Jewish guy.
He said, tomorrow, I'll come over.
I'll bring you some goodies, some chewing gum, some chocolate, from down at PX.
Sure enough, the next day, he knew already he had here a dozen or so, 15 Jewish people.
He came in with that backpack, chewing gum and chocolate-- all real stuff that we couldn't imagine that will ever see in our lives.
However, this guy brought it over-- Walter Schrader.
And he was there with us, and I told him, you know, I have to tell you something.
In Yiddish, I spoke to him.
Not far from here, the Germans machine-gunned our group.
And I have an idea, I said.
If you take me with a tank to the road, I can pinpoint where it was.
He said, you sure?
I said, I feel confident that I can recognize the area.
So I remember when we walked, we came to a point, I said, left into the woods.
So we went there.
He said to me, I'll come over with the tank, take you in the tank, go-- and we'll go there.
Because Germans are still hiding in the woods.
It's not safe to walk on foot.
I said, that's fine.
He came over with the tank.
And we go-- I saw it's the same road, identical same road.
However, we came from the opposite direction with his tank.
And I sat on the left-hand side.
I go on the left-hand side, I see nothing.
I say, wait a minute.
I walk back on the road and take a look on the right-hand side and I see what I told you before-- pots, cups to drink.
And I see it scattered there.
I said, look, we marched into the woods from this side, there was a left.
You came with a-- with the tank from this side and it was a right.
Obviously, I misled you.
I said, this is this area.
He said, hold it.
This is freshly-dug.
You recognize in woods when earth was moved.
As a soldier-- you know, by me it's no difference, young man.
He says, this was freshly-dug.
He went over to the tank-- they had shovels.
On the tank, with two straps holding it.
He took over one-- he told me, he said, can you dig?
I said, sure.
He gave me one, he took one.
He said, where do you think it was?
I said, right here, right here, this spot.
I'm telling you, that's where we were sitting.
And I step on the shovel-- a half a shovel, I hit the body.
He said, that's all.
That's all.
Back on the tank.
He took us back to the-- where we were in our camp.
Took us back-- he went to the [NON-ENGLISH]-- it means like the mayor of the town.
He said, you got here people buried.
He said, maybe six inches of soil.
Get them out of there if you want to live.
And give them a decent burial.
He said-- because he told us later what he did.
Anyway, I don't know what day it was, because I lost already count of the days.
Shortly-- two days or three days later.
He came in and he told me, there's going to be a ceremony, a burial-- a burial memory something prayer is going to be said for people that-- because seven kilometers from this town, from Yavinitz.
In Gardelegen they also took in a group of prisoners into a barn, circled it around with barbed wire, poured gasoline around, lit it up and shot with machine guns, and they burned everybody.
So he said, these people-- they made the Germans take out, and they created a regular cemetery-- crosses.
And every ten crosses, there was a Star of David also on a stick sticking out of the ground.
That was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like they made [NON-ENGLISH] they make for the dead.
There was a chaplain.
Then, I hear over the loudspeaker, [NON-ENGLISH].
And I figured it was a Hebrew chaplain-- then, we call it a rabbi, whatever.
And we knew they gave these people from there, they had to bring here, to Gardelegen.
And they were buried in a general cemetery.
And we were free.
Say it again?
From the camp-- I don't know how long we spent there.
May.
May, maybe June.
They started to put us in bigger camps, with more people.
And then came a time when the Russians and the Americans had to exchange what they promised each other, the borders-- how far one will be, how far-- and our area became Russian area.
So the Americans put trucks by us and our home.
So they gave us civilian buildings-- beautiful, it would look beautiful if the Americans-- and they went to the [NON-ENGLISH], the mayor, and told him, give these people plenty of food.
And that Volvek Weiner, the one I told you what his brother was looking for him-- he was a few years older.
And he was already in the ghetto, working in an office.
And he knew how to organize things.
So he went to the [NON-ENGLISH] and he told him, I am the leader of these people.
And the Americans told him, give him plenty.
And he said, we need so-and-so much of meat a day, per person.
So-and-so much flour, so-and-so much butter, you know, so-and-so much bread.
And the mayor, OK, whatever you want, you'll get.
And we were pretty good off there.
I don't remember how long it took, we were there.
But anyway, before the armies exchanged the zones, the American zone became Russian zone.
They had to straighten out the borders.
They put trucks to us, the Americans.
Whoever wants to leave, come to the American zone, where it's established, can come.
I wanted to know exactly what happened to my family.
I said, no, I'll stay here.
Here is closer.
I'll go back to Poland, take a look if anybody survived.
I knew my brother, from the letter, that he died in the ghetto of starvation.
But I was looking for my mother.
I had cousins in the ghetto.
And I go back.
Anyway, we hitched rides by train.
We walked.
I came to Lodz.
I went through the Polish border, Polish soldiers asked.
We told them, we are from the concentration camp.
We are Polish citizens.
We want to go back home, see if anybody survived.
Open the thing, let us through, no problem.
Then, we hitched rides, like I said, on a train.
We traveled on the roof of a train.
Finally, we came to Lodz.
Lodz-- meet people on the street, could recognize Jewish people.
Where is the Jewish organization?
The [NON-ENGLISH].
So somebody told me, on this, are you familiar?
I said, I was born in Lodz.
He said, [NON-ENGLISH]-- he named the street, that-- that number, you go to an organization.
You can go on.
Fine, I went on.
By the way, that fellow Alta went to the American.
I went to Poland, so we're separated.
I was on my own.
I went to Poland, came to Lodz, and went to the [NON-ENGLISH].
And there was a person there inside with a little window.
And I said, I just came here and I want to find out if any relatives survived.
What do you know?
You've got lists?
She says, yeah, outside on the wall, you got lists of people who survived.
I found a cousin of mine-- his name, and his sister, another cousin of mine, and that they are in Germany, in Feldafing, near Munich.
It was a camp.
So I know already, Jacob [NON-ENGLISH] survived, that they're there.
But I saw the name also for my brother, the youngest, Abraham.
That he is in Lodz.
And I said, I see here-- my name is Goteiner.
And this Abraham is my half-brother.
How can I find his address, where he is?
Because it doesn't say he's in a camp in Germany.
She says, one minute.
It doesn't say there that he is in Lodz.
And she opened a book and she told me.
He lives [NON-ENGLISH] 14.
I said, fine.
I walked from there and I came in [NON-ENGLISH] 14.
And [NON-ENGLISH] 14 was the place where I told you the factory from the salamis, the bologna kitchen.
And he worked there in that factory, and we bought their stuff.
So I come to the yard-- you know, in Europe, you had yards.
The houses were three sides surrounded.
You had a gate in front.
And you walk in the yard.
And I knew where the factory was, because I went to pick up stuff there always.
And I go in and I see my brother works and mixes.
I say, [INAUDIBLE].
He says, I took the Yamonds' factory.
Nobody-- [COUGHS] excuse me-- survived from Yamond.
I took it and we got a few guys, wurst-makers, and we make and we're selling.
I says, can I work here?
Because I had an idea how to do it just from looking, from I used to come there-- [COUGHS] just from looking.
He said, sure, you can have a job here.
So we all lived.
We took the Yamonds' apartment.
So they took the apartment there-- him with another fellow.
Now, I was here.
They had a few workers that they worked with.
He says, you can stay with me.
So I was there a couple months, two months, three months.
One day, I go out in the street, lunchtime, you know, lunch break, go out in the street, and I hear machine-gun fire.
And not soldiers, civilians.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I ran in back, but later, we start talking-- what happened here?
You wouldn't be familiar-- you would be familiar with that name, but the AK-- it was AK, two initials.
It was in Polish-- meant Armia Krajowa, Home Army.
These were civilians who were against the Russians, even though the Russians liberated Poland.
Was nothing to do, and against Jews.
And they were shooting at Jews, in Lodz, in the street.
When I heard that the AK was shooting Jews, I said to my brother, I have no place here.
I'm leaving back to Germany.
I said, I don't want to be killed here.
I survived this war.
I don't want to be killed.
He said, go ahead if you want to.
So we got together a group of people, and we smuggled ourselves back to Germany, to the American zone.
And I went in the area where I saw my cousin survived, Jacob Alexander and Malk Alexander, to that camp.
I asked, where's Feldafing?
And they gave me directions and survived.
And I walked there toward that camp.
Did you ever find out what happened to your mother?
Yes, yes.
I forgot to mention that episode.
While we were in Gardelegen, where the Americans were still-- this is going back before the Russians took over this area.
We found out that there was a camp 40 kilometers away, there's a camp with Jewish girls, only women, from the Lodz ghetto.
So we got some bicycles, a few guys-- [NON-ENGLISH], a few guys-- and we went to look for these Jewish girls, to Salzwedel.
We traveled by foot, hills, up-- it was a hard job.
But we came there, we arrived.
And I asked people, where are people from the Lodz ghetto?
And they point in this building.
I went in there and I found neighbors.
We used to live, before they dispossessed us-- where I told you after I got the job.
We used to live [NON-ENGLISH], 2, number 2.
They moved me as superintendent to [NON-ENGLISH] 20, a few hours away.
However, at [NON-ENGLISH] 4, the next building to ours, used to be-- the guy used to make taps from shoes, a manufacturer.
And his son and his daughter, they knew me.
We had the deli right next to their store, or only two stores away, and they knew me.
And I said, you-- did you see my mother?
And when they evacuated the ghetto to Auschwitz, they took houses, areas.
And these two went on the same train.
So this is the one that told me, your mother went to the wrong side where they didn't go to the camp.
So there, I found that my mother perished in Auschwitz after they liquidated the Lodz ghetto, in September.
That was about six months after they took me away.
They liquidated the total ghetto, because the Russians were approaching there.
So from-- from that woman, I found that my mother was killed, somehow in Auschwitz.
And you were telling about when you were-- you had left your brother in Poland to go to Germany and to find your cousins.
And then what happened after that?
OK, before I reached that camp-- Feldafing was the name where my cousins, when I saw them on the list, that's where they resided.
That's where they were.
I had to pass through another camp, also with people, Fohrenwald.
And I said, I'm at a camp here.
Let me first go in, get something to eat.
And then I'll have strength to walk-- to go to Feldafing.
I asked people, how far is Feldafing?
They said, it's only a few kilometers, not bad.
Coming into that camp, I found some people that were with us in Gardelegen, that left the American zone.
Some-- not all, but some.
And they said, how you doing?
So I said, fine.
I joined here the police, the camp police.
We had food, we had a pretty nice-- we get paid a little every month.
You know, the Americans want we should keep order.
And I was there maybe a week.
And they told me that I mentioned Volvek Weiner, that the guy was an organizer, he went to the mayor in Gardelegen, told him we want food.
That there's another camp, Zeilsheim, near Frankfurt.
And this Volvek is the president from that camp.
I said to myself, let me first see my cousins.
But I'm not settling anywhere.
Volvek knows me pretty good.
I'll go there, I'll get a job, and I'll be fine.
So I left Fohrenwald for Feldafing, I met my cousin, you know, with his brother.
And spent there, I don't know, two days, three days.
And I said, I've got to go to Zeilsheim.
I have a dear friend there, that he can do for me-- because my cousin worked with the girls in the kitchen.
And they also got enough food-- there wasn't a problem after the war, so much, the food.
And I left there for-- for Zeilsheim.
And sure enough, I came to the camp and I asked, where's Mr.
Weiner live?
They showed me, I knocked on the door.
I came-- oh, Henrik, happy we meet.
I said, well, look, I would like you to-- you should register me in this camp.
He said, you went to Poland.
I said, yes, I went to Poland.
It didn't work out there.
And I told him the story with the AK, they're shooting machine-guns on the street, Jews.
I said, it's not for me and I came back.
What do you want to do?
I said, I don't care.
Give me any job.
I should get an extra ration, we should have enough-- he said, do you want to join the police?
They'll train you here, we have, you know, people who can-- I said, I don't care.
He said, we'll make you a policeman.
I said, assign me an apartment.
He gave me an apartment, they assigned an apartment then gave me the police.
And the police had already-- beside what we get, the rations from the camp-- you know, because we used to get rations there, food.
But it wasn't a starvation ration-- a starvation ration-- we got already enough food.
Beside that, we used to have an open casino for the police that we used to go and they eat breakfast, lunch, and dinner.
So I had double food, so it was already fine, thanks to Volvek Weiner.
And then, after, I don't know, a few months, my half-brother from Lodz, who had the factory, arrives.
He knew-- found out that I am in Zeilsheim near Frankfurt.
I said, what are you doing here?
He said, you know what?
I got a letter, a letter in the mail, signed AK, to leave this place within 24 hours.
If I don't leave it, they'll take me out dead.
And that's what they used to do.
He had a factory.
Somebody wanted the factory, a Polish fellow.
They went to the AK, get this Jew out of here.
And sure enough, that's how he arrived in that camp.
So now, I was a policeman.
I have good food, fine.
My brother says, Henry, I'm going to open a factory here, make wurst, kielbasa.
I said, fine.
I said, we'll work together.
He says, no problem.
Because he already learned-- I had learned some, I worked with him.
And we-- in the house where I was assigned to was a basement.
It was really like a washroom.
In Europe, when you wash your clothes, you cook them in a kettle to kill the lice, or whatever there.
You cook them while you wash them in the soap.
I called German bricklayers.
I said, this corner, I want you build me a smoke-house.
Connect the chimney to go for the outside.
And I went to a mechanic to make me doors, you know, for-- because we knew exactly what we want.
All we had to tell him-- and they did.
We built the smoke house.
We washed out the kettle that they washed the clothes.
Scrubbed it with steel wool, everything available, because it was copper.
It was like shining brand-new.
This, we needed to cook, because we used to smoke and cook the salami, both.
And we opened there a little factory.
Made salami, sold it, made a few bucks and made a decent living.
And then came a time when they said we have to liquidate the camp.
We want everybody to disperse, you know, three years elapsed.
Three years elapsed.
Oh, hey, I'm going three years.
OK.
